coredata.dc:description: There has been significant interest in the advancement of photocatalytic materials that can respond to visible light, in order to fulfill the needs of conserving energy and reducing emissions. Photocatalysis is a method employed to eliminate organic contaminants from wastewater. This study examined the effectiveness of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), zinc oxide/graphene quantum dots (ZnO/GQDs), and zinc oxide/graphene quantum dots/cadmium selenide (ZnO/GQDs/CdSe) as catalyst powders in decomposing Paracetamol and Ciprofloxacin antibiotics when exposed to visible light. The ZnO/GQDs/CdSe composite was synthesized using calcination at a temperature of 400 °C for a duration of 2 h. The studies demonstrated that ZnO/GQDs/CdSe exhibited exceptional catalytic efficiency in the photodegradation of Paracetamol and Ciprofloxacin antibiotic, surpassing other catalysts with degradation rates of 99.2 % and 99.9 %, respectively. The ZnO/GQDs/CdSe composite can be recycled up to 5 times without experiencing any additional damage. Our findings indicate that the combination of GQDs, ZnO, and CdSe forms a photocatalytic system that follows the Z-scheme heterojunction model. This system demonstrates enhanced efficiency in transferring photogenerated electron-hole pairs and possesses a large redox capacity. These conclusions are supported by the results of radical trapping tests.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic performance with electrochemical effects for ZnO/Graphene Quantum Dots/CdSe nanocomposite toward ciprofloxacin antibiotics and paracetamol degradation under visible light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There has been significant interest in the advancement of photocatalytic materials that can respond to visible light, in order to fulfill the needs of conserving energy and reducing emissions. Photocatalysis is a method employed to eliminate organic contaminants from wastewater. This study examined the effectiveness of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), zinc oxide/graphene quantum dots (ZnO/GQDs), and zinc oxide/graphene quantum dots/cadmium selenide (ZnO/GQDs/CdSe) as catalyst powders in decomposing Paracetamol and Ciprofloxacin antibiotics when exposed to visible light. The ZnO/GQDs/CdSe composite was synthesized using calcination at a temperature of 400 °C for a duration of 2 h. The studies demonstrated that ZnO/GQDs/CdSe exhibited exceptional catalytic efficiency in the photodegradation of Paracetamol and Ciprofloxacin antibiotic, surpassing other catalysts with degradation rates of 99.2 % and 99.9 %, respectively. The ZnO/GQDs/CdSe composite can be recycled up to 5 times without experiencing any additional damage. Our findings indicate that the combination of GQDs, ZnO, and CdSe forms a photocatalytic system that follows the Z-scheme heterojunction model. This system demonstrates enhanced efficiency in transferring photogenerated electron-hole pairs and possesses a large redox capacity. These conclusions are supported by the results of radical trapping tests.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: There has been significant apprehension over the past several years over the appearance of pollutants in the environment, as the effects induced by these substances and their existence in the ecosystem are often uncertain. Pharmaceuticals, which are widely used by humans, are among the most concerning new pollutants when they infiltrate the ecosystem [1–4,89]. Phenolic chemicals are regarded as important organic pollutants that contribute to environmental risks. Many widely used medications and pesticides include phenolic compounds [5–7]. For this study, we have selected paracetamol, often known as paracetamol, and the antibiotic Ciprofloxacin as our research substances. The discovery and use of penicillin, the inaugural antibiotic, occurred throughout the 1940s. Subsequently, several antibiotics and associated medications have been produced and employed [8–10]. The advent of antibiotics has ushered in significant progress in the medical treatment of infectious illnesses [11,12]. Furthermore, antibiotics are extensively employed for the purpose of treating infections and preventing illnesses in animals. They are also utilized for production purposes in the domains of animal husbandry, poultry, aquaculture, and agriculture [13,14]. Nevertheless, the improper use and unregulated administration of these medications have led to the presence of antibiotic residues in the water [15]. The existence of these substances in the environment can cause significant repercussions on ecosystems, such as disturbance of ecological equilibrium, threats to water quality, and heightened antibiotic resistance in bacteria, even at minimal concentrations. Antibiotic compounds are released into the aquatic environment through many means, with medical antibiotic waste being the predominant source. Residues of antibiotics present in soil and aquatic habitats have the potential to pollute the food chain, including vegetables and fish, as well as drinking water, so exposing animals and people to these compounds. Hence, conducting research and study on the eradication of antibiotics in aqueous settings is crucial [16,17]. Ciprofloxacin (CPF), which was released in 1987, is categorized as a fluoroquinolone antibiotic and is extensively employed in animal husbandry owing to its wide-ranging efficacy. CPF has deleterious effects similar to other antibiotics that have the potential to accumulate throughout the body [18,19]. Paracetamol is a commonly utilized medication with analgesic and antipyretic properties. It is introduced into the environment either by unaltered excretion in urine and faces or through a process of transformation that results in the production of hazardous metabolites, such as N-Acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQ) [20–23]. These metabolites are introduced into the food chain via water-based environments. Multiple studies have demonstrated that prolonged exposure to paracetamol has chronic impacts on aquatic species, namely affecting their growth rate and reproductive capacity [24–33]. Residues of paracetamol have been detected in potable water, potentially leading to an individual above the acceptable daily dosage [34–36]. In order to mitigate the presence of PCT and antibiotic medication pollution, many solutions have been suggested, such as implementing legislative modifications, enhancing the Environmental Risk Assessment (ERA) process, and imposing restrictions on emissions, particularly for pharmaceuticals classified as hazardous waste [37,38]. Nevertheless, these precautions prove insufficient, leading to the accumulation of developing contaminants in the environment. Consequently, Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) that may break down dissolved organic contaminants have been created and implemented in wastewater treatment facilities. AOP is distinguished by the generation of hydroxyl radicals, which possess the ability to oxidize nearly all organic compounds, resulting in the formation of carbon dioxide (CO2) and water (H2O) [39–42]. Furthermore, there has been a proliferation of strategies aimed at enhancing photocatalytic processes through the utilization of Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), Zinc Oxide (ZnO) and Cadmium Selenium (CdSe). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanoscale fragments of graphene that exhibit unique optical and electronic properties, such as strong light absorption and high electron mobility. GQDs are significant in photocatalysis as they enhance light absorption across a broad spectrum, including visible light, thereby improving the efficiency of the photocatalytic process. Furthermore, they serve as highly efficient electron acceptors and transporters, thereby minimizing the recombination of electron-hole pairs and enhancing the overall catalytic activity. Moreover, GQDs provide a significant surface area and create active sites, thereby improving their interaction with contaminants and facilitating more effective degradation [82,83]. ZnO is a semiconductor material characterized by a wide bandgap, which enhances its efficacy in photocatalytic applications, particularly when exposed to UV light. Photocatalysis is significant due to its ability to generate electron-hole pairs upon light absorption, leading to the production of reactive oxygen species such as hydroxyl and superoxide radicals that facilitate the degradation of pollutants. Furthermore, ZnO demonstrates stability, cost-effectiveness, and significant oxidizing capabilities, which contribute to its extensive application in environmental remediation and water purification efforts [84–86].CdSe is a semiconductor characterized by a narrow bandgap, enabling it to effectively absorb visible light, which enhances its applicability in photocatalysis under sunlight conditions. The material plays a significant role in photocatalysis by generating electron-hole pairs upon exposure to light, which facilitate the production of reactive species for the degradation of pollutants. CdSe plays a significant role in improving the photocatalytic efficiency of materials by broadening light absorption into the visible spectrum, thereby complementing UV-absorbing semiconductors such as ZnO [87,88].

The objective of this study is to produce ZnO/GQDs/CdSe compounds by the calcination process at a temperature of 400 °C for a duration of 12 h. The synthesized substance underwent characterization and identification to determine its photocatalytic activity in the elimination of Ciprofloxacin and Paracetamol. The comparison with previous study also shown in Table 1 . The study has discovered the impact of ciprofloxacin concentration, catalyst dose, pH, and reuse of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe, as well as the suggested mechanism.

All chemical reagent in this study is Analytical Grade. (Zinc acetate dihydrate(Zn(CH3COO)2) (Sigma-Aldrich) (CAS: 5970-45-6), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) (Sigma-Aldrich) (CAS: 1310-73-2), natural graphite (Sigma-Aldrich) (CAS: 7782-42-5), sulfuric acid (H2SO4) (Sigma-Aldrich) (CAS: 7664-93-9), potassium permanganate (KMnO4)(Duksan Pure Chemicals Co.) (CAS: 7722-64-7), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (Duksan Pure Chemicals Co.), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 10 %)(Duksan Pure Chemicals Co.) (CAS: 7647-01-0), Photodegradation methods in this research using 3 types of the visible light (Geni LED 15W and 30W, Philips HalogenA 75W), VCX 750 model of an Ultrasonicator.

2.19 g of Zinc acetate dihydrate were dissolved in 100 ml of deionized water with stirring, whereas 8 g of NaOH were dissolved in 10 ml of deionized water. Once the solution is thoroughly mixed, gradually add NaOH to the solution containing zinc acetate dihydrate until the pH 11. This should be done while stirring the solution and at a temperature of 70 °C. Subsequently, ethanol was employed to eliminate impurities, resulting in the formation of a white precipitate. The surfactant ingredient, namely 0.55 g of CТАB, was combined with 10 ml of distilled water by stirring. Following the precipitation of ZnO, the CTAB solution was combined with ZnO nanoparticles while stirring. Subsequently, subjected to a drying process at a temperature of 100 °C in a Oven for a duration of 24 h. The materials obtained from the synthesis process were subsequently subjected to calcination at a temperature of 500 °C for a duration of 2 h [79].

The Hummer–Offeman method was utilized to synthesize graphene by the oxidation of graphite powder. A mixture of 15 g of natural graphite and 450 mL of sulfuric acid (H2SO4) was prepared by combining them in de-ionized water. The mixture was then agitated for 1 h at a temperature of 0 °C in an ice bath. Afterwards, the ice bath was removed, and potassium permanganate (45 g, KMnO4) was slowly added to the solution (graphite + H2SO4) while continuously stirring at a temperature of 35 °C, until the solution obtained a little reddish tint. The container was securely sealed and kept at a temperature of 1 °C while being agitated for a period of 30 min. Following that, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was incrementally introduced for a duration of 5 min. Afterwards, the solution was subjected to several rinses using acetone and hydrochloric acid (HCl, 10 %). Subsequently, the product underwent thermal processing in an oven at a temperature of 90 °C for a period of 12 h, leading to the creation of graphite oxide powder. The graphite oxide powder, in its initial state, was added to a 200 mL amount of de-ionized water. The mixture was aggressively agitated for 30 min and then subjected to continuous ultrasonication for 2 h using the. Ultimately, the solution was purified and underwent many rounds of hot water washing. Afterwards, the sample was subjected to a 6-h heat treatment in an oven, leading to the production of graphene oxide powder [58].
5 g of Graphite Oxide mixed with 5 % NaOH, synthesis by using hydrothermal for 180 °C, 12 h, after that wash with DI water several times, dry in the oven 70 °C 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of zinc oxide-graphene quantum dots (ZnO/GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: 1 g of ZnO mixed with 100 ml DI water, stir in the room temperature for 1 h, after that add 5 g of GQDs powder and stir for 1 h, after mixing in the room temperature, move to hydrothermal tank and synthesis under condition 180 °C for 12 h, after that dry in the 60 °C 12 h, and grin to become powder after that the powder has been synthesis for calcination in condition 400 °C, 4 h.

The preparation of the CdSe materials involved a series of steps. Firstly, 0.4 mmol of selenium powder was combined with 80 ml of ethylenediamine and heated at 200 °C for 2 h. This resulted in the solution turning black in color. Next, 1 g of cadmium acetate dehydrate was added and stirred for 1 h until a homogeneous mixture was achieved. Finally, 1 g of ZnO-GQDs was added to the solution while continuously stirring to create a uniform solution. Subsequently, the blended solution was transferred into a Petri dish and subjected to a drying process for a duration of 24 h within an oven maintained at a temperature of 80 °C. During this process, the solution solidified into a compact mass, exhibiting a distinct black hue. Subsequently, a mortar was employed to pulverize the solid material for a duration of 30 min till it transformed into a fine powder. The powder was placed inside a tube furnace and subjected to calcination temperatures of 400 °C for a duration of 2 h in a closed system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoactivity; photodegradation of ciprofloxacin antibiotic and paracetamol under visible light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para:
By following Nugroho et al. [43]. The catalyst's photoactivity was assessed by the degradation of Ciprofloxacin and Paracetamol. A quantity of 50 mg of the manufactured photocatalyst was combined with a pollutant solution that had been made to have a final volume of 200 mL and a final concentration of 10 mg L−1. After adding the catalyst powder to the pollutant, the sample was mix in a dark room for 60 min to reach the equilibrium point. Following this, visible light was turned on under different conditions (15, 30 and 75W). A 5 ml sample was collected after various mixing times (10, 20, 30, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 180 and 240 min) and then centrifuged at 4000 RPM for 10 min. The concentration of the samples was analyzed using the UV–vis spectrophotometric (Uv–Vis Spectro Optizen POP) method at wavelengths of 243 nm (Paracetamol) and 554 nm (ciprofloxacin), respectively.
(1) Photodegradation efficiency (%) = (1-C/C0) x 100
where C0 and C denote the starting concentration and the concentration of the pollutant in the aqueous solution after a certain period of irradiation, respectively. The degrading response of the pollutant by photocatalysis follows a first-order kinetic model, as seen below.
(2) ln (C0/C) = k1t
where k1 is the first order rate constant. On examining pollutant degradation, the influence of some experimental parameters including the initial solution pH, photocatalyst loading, and initial pollutant concentration on photoactivity was investigated [44,45].

The crystal structure of the nanocomposites was analyzed by the XRD (SHIMADZU XRD-6000) equipped with a Cu Ka X-ray source (1.5406 Å). The shape of the particles and morphology was examined by the TEM (Hitachi H9500, Tokyo, Japan) and SEM (SEM; JSM-5600 JEOL, Akishima, Tokyo, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) (Bruker TENSOR 27). The elemental compositions and bonding states of the sample were evaluated by the XPS (PHI 5000 Versa Probe) with a monochromatized Al Ka X-ray source (10 kV, 1500 W, pass energy = 40 eV). The bandgap energy of the samples was calibrated by using the Kubelka-Munk function that used the UV–Vis DRS data (SHIMADZU UV-2600). The PL analysis (Versa-STAT-3), the analysis conducted at room temperature by using th 514 nm laser light source with ND 10 % filter between 550 nm and 900 nm. The resistivity of the samples was examined by EIS (electrochemical impedance spectra) using an electrochemical analyzer (Zahner, Germany). The photocurrent was measured by utilizing an SLS 301-stabilized tabletop Tungsten-Halogen light source equipped with a 150 W Halogen lamp. The wavelength spans from 360 to 2700 nm. The output optical power exceeds 1.6 W.

Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) investigation was used to assess the chemical functionalization of the catalyst powder (Fig. 1 (a)). The ZnO bonding may be identified by the peaks at 1405 and 1407 cm−1, as seen in Fig. 1(a) [46,47]. The intense peaks seen at 1005, 1037 and 1045 cm−1 can be attributed to the strong bonding between carbon and oxygen [43,48]. Additionally, the peak at 738 cm-1 corresponds to the stretching of the Cd–Se bond [49]. The XRD result was analyst for determine the crystal structure of catalyst powder, and it's shown in Fig. 1(b), the full spectrum of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe is matched with the crystal reference, the ZnO/GQDs has the average diameter crystal 19.57 nm and ZnO/GQDs/CdSe have the diameter crystal 19.66 nm respectively (Table 3
) The average diameter crystal was calculated using the Debye-Scherrer formula [50,51], (3)
(3) D = 0.9λ / βcosθ
The catalyst powder has been analyzed using the Raman shift method, revealing peaks in the specified range (Fig. S5). 208 cm−1 (Lo CdSe) [52], 327 cm−1 (2E2 Mode of ZnO) [53], 437 cm−1 (E2 Mode of ZnO), and 530 cm−1 (E1 Mode of ZnO) [54] respectively. Fig. 1(c) illustrates that pure graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess a broad band gap energy, whereas ZnO/GQDs exhibit reduced values of band gap energy. The hybridization of graphene with ZnO and CdSe in ZnO/GQDs/CdSe results in the narrowest band gap energy values. Furthermore, by following Energy Wavelength equation [55,56], the band gap energy of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe experiences a substantial decrease to 2.81 eV. This decrease is attributed to the successful reduction of the band gap energy and the adjustment of the energy level location in the ternary hybrid nanocomposite. The ZnO/GQDs/CdSe composite has a high ability to absorb light, leading to the formation of electron-hole pairs and thus enhancing its photocatalytic activity. This augmentation is particularly notable when exposed to low-energy visible light. Ultimately, a combination of several semiconductors can serve as a more effective charge carrier by creating narrow band gap energy values at equilibrium energy levels, thanks to the optical characteristics of the corresponding nanocomposites. The combination of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe is anticipated to demonstrate exceptional performance in harnessing visible light, primarily owing to their high quantum efficiency. The creation of binary or ternary hybrid nanocomposites is regarded as a highly successful approach to augment catalytic activity, especially under conditions of low light energy. The method of effective light energy transfer involves lowering the band gap energy and maximizing the potential of charge carriers, hence increasing the overall efficiency of photocatalysis. The primary focus of this research is to implement a systematic control method to decrease the band gap energy.

The semiconducting characteristics of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe were assessed by calculating the flat-band potential (EFB) using the Mott-Schottky plot. The whole spectrum of the Mott-Schottky plot may be shown in Fig. 1(d). The Mott-Schottky drawings illustrate the relationship between the EFB values of the NCs and the vs. Ag/AgCl reference electrode. The estimated potentials may be found in Table 2 [57]. Fig. 1(d) demonstrates that the particle size of the catalyst powder is influenced by the semiconducting type and bandgap. Particles with a size more than 5 nm exhibit n-type behavior, while particles with a size smaller than 5 nm exhibit p-type behavior [58]. The computed EFB data were utilized to derive the ECB and EVB, mostly for the purpose of calculating theoretical phenomena. As outlined in Table 2, this method is employed to identify the possible locations of H2O/O2 and to establish the process by which charge carriers are transported in the ZnO/GQDs/CdSe nanocomposites. The H2O/O2 reduction potential has a standard redox potential of 1.23 V compared to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE). The radical dot OH/H2O has a standard redox potential of 2.3 V compared to the NHE. The electrochemical potential of GQDs, ZnO/GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs/CdSe, relative to the standard NHE, Table 2, Ecb/V vs. NHE of GQDs, ZnO/GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs/CdSe is 0.076, 0.0256 and −0.024 respectively, and Ecb/V vs. NHE of GQDs, ZnO/GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs/CdSe is 3.126, 3.216, and 2.786 respectively. SEM-EDs image of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe showed in Fig. 1(e), the atomic% under EDS it's shown 39 % (C atom), 16 % (O atom), 12 % (Cd atom), 12 % (Se atom), and 15 % (Zn atom) respectively. The PL spectra shown in Fig. 1(f) indicate that the ZnO/GQDs/CdSe photocatalyst had the lowest intensity compared to the other experimental conditions. This suggests that ZnO/GQDs/CdSe had the slowest rate of charge carrier recombination.

The elemental composition and chemical oxidation state of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe at the surface were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The spectrum depicted in Fig. 2 . The survey scan spectra presented in Fig. 2(a) illustrate the existence of Cd, Se, Zn, O, and C elements inside the ZnO/GQDs/CdSe. The spectra shown in Fig. 2(b) underwent deconvolution, which revealed the presence of O1s at positions of 530.5 eV, 531.2 eV, and 532.5 eV These positions correspond to the OH, CO, and C–OH states, respectively [59–62]. Through deconvolution analysis of the C1s spectrum shown in Fig. 2(c), it was discovered that this spectrum contains a single distinct peak at 284.15 eV. The presence of C–C was responsible for these peaks [63]. Fig. 2(d) demonstrates the separation of the two clear peaks that come from the original ZnO material. The data was analyzed using deconvolution, which revealed two distinct peaks at 1021.3 eV and 1045.1 eV. These peaks can be attributed to the presence of Zn2P3/2 and Zn2P1/2, respectively [43,45]. The deconvolution of Se3d yielded two peaks at 59.04 eV and 55.19 eV, as depicted in Fig. 2(e) [64,65]. The energy levels of 404.7eV and 412.3 eV (Fig. 2(f)) are associated with Cd3d5/2 and Cd3d3/2, respectively [64,65]. The ZnO/GQDs/CdSe particle has been analyzed by using HR-TEM, and it's shows in Fig. 2(g) and (f), The TEM image displays a composite of three chemical compounds: GQDsand it'sd CdSe, performing a reaction. The HRTEM images reveal lattice fringe distances of 0.13 nm for GQDs, 0.25 nm for ZnO, and 0.38 nm for CdSe in the ZnO/GQDs/CdSe structure, respectively.

The composites were analyzed for their conductivity, separation, and movement of electrons and holes using several electrochemical tests including cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), Tafel plots, chronoamperometry, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and photocurrent response. The data is displayed in Fig. 3 . The EIS Nyquist analysis was performed to assess the impedance between the electrolyte and electrode surface. Fig. 3(a) displays the whole EIS spectrum. The magnitude of the semicircle's radius serves as an indication of resistance. A larger semicircle radius corresponds to a higher resistance between the electrolyte and electrode surface, a decrease in charge transfer resistance signifies enhanced efficiency in the separation of electron-hole pairs formed at the interface between the photocatalyst and the electrolyte. Efficient charge separation is vital as it decreases the chances of recombination, thereby improving photocatalytic activity [66]. This behavior was seen in the case of pure graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The arc radius decreased dramatically in the following order: GQDs > ZnO/GQDs > ZnO/GQDs/CdSe. The ZnO/GQDs/CdSe structure exhibited a minimal arc radius, indicating efficient separation of charge pairs and electron migration towards the electrode surface. Additionally, this structure had the lowest charge transfer resistance for photo-induced charges. In addition, this study investigated the effects of PCT and CPF on the EIS test at various concentrations (25, 50, and 75 ppm). The results, as depicted in Figs. S1(a–c), demonstrate that increased concentrations of CPF and PCT result in reduced electronic transmission capacities. This is relevant to both CPF and PCT. Furthermore, the charge transfer resistances of the electrodes are evaluated by the use of LSV (Fig. 3(b)), Chronoamperometry Test (Fig. 3(c)) and Tafel plot (Fig. 3(d)), Fig. 3(b) and (c) display the outcomes of linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and Chronoamperometry test, illustrating the curves of ZnO, ZnO/GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs/CdSe. The oxidation current measured for the ZnO/GQDs/CdSe electrode exceeds that of the other two electrodes and corresponds to the findings obtained from linear sweep voltammetry. The results illustrate the synergistic effect of incorporating ZnO, GQDs, and CdSe into the mixed oxides ZnO/GQDs/CdSe. It demonstrates how the improved performance of ZnO, GQDs, and CdSe is due to the material's capacity to produce and segregate charge carriers (electrons and holes) effectively in the presence of light, increasing the rates of photocatalytic processes. As a result, the material can withstand higher photocurrent under operational settings, which increases its usefulness in applications including water splitting, pollutant degradation, and solar fuel production. In addition, the corrosion potential of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe was evaluated using PCT and CPF in the LSV (linear sweep voltammetry) and chronoamperometry tests. These tests are illustrated in Figs. S1(c–d) for the LSV and Figs. S2(a–c) for the Chronoamperometry test. The findings suggest that both CPF and PCT can increase the corrosion potential of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe in the LSV test [58,67]. Fig. 3(d) illustrates that all working electrodes exhibit electro-positive corrosion potential, which is characterized by low rates of corrosion and efficient charge transfer. Moreover, the observation of increased cathodic and anodic Tafel slope values in ZnO/GQDs/CdSe suggests that these working electrodes display improved protection against corrosion. Fig. S2 illustrates that the presence of PCT and CPF on the working electrode might increase the corrosion potential of the ZnO/GQDs/CdSe. This effect may be attributed to the adsorption of molecules onto the metal surface. The process of adsorption can alter the surface characteristics of the metal, hence impacting its electrochemical activity [68]. The presence of the adsorbed CPF and PCT layer can serve as a barrier, hence altering the rate at which corrosion occurs [69]. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) characteristics of GQDs, ZnO/GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs/CdSe were evaluated and compared, as depicted in Fig. 3(e). The specific capacitance values for GQDs, ZnO/GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs/CdSe were measured at a scanning rate of 10 m/Vs and are displayed in Table 4 using a specified calculation method [70].
(4) Cp = A / 2mk(V2 – V1)

The capacitance or Cp has been calculated by using formula (4), while where A represents the area under the CV curve (AV), m is the material mass, k is the CV scan rate in volts per second, and (V2 – V1) is the potential window of the CV. The specific capacitance of the ZnO/GQDs/CdSe composite is recorded at 1.20 Fg-1. Nevertheless, the areal specific capacitance of ZnO/GQDs was 0.95 Fg-1, which is 0.08 times less than that of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe. In comparison, the specific capacitance of GQDs was 0.98 Fg-1, indicating a 0.81-fold decrease compared to ZnO/GQDs/CdSe. Furthermore, it is apparent that ZnO/GQDs/CdSe exhibits enhanced conductivity and a larger surface area. The highest capacitance in the CV test, it's indicated the separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs are improved by high capacitance, which denotes a wide surface area and plenty of active sites for charge accumulation. This property raises the overall efficiency of photocatalytic activities, including water splitting or pollutant degradation, by lowering recombination rates. The effect of CPF and PCT at various concentrations had an impact on the specific capacitance and conductivity of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe, as shown in Figs. S2(g–i). The nanocomposites' light sensitivity was further assessed using the transient photocurrent response, as depicted in Fig. 3(f). The ZnO/GQDs/CdSe composite demonstrated a favorable increase in photocurrent upon light exposure, surpassing the performance of other materials. This indicates a successful segregation of the electrons and holes created by light in the ZnO/GQDs/CdSe system, The photocurrent response observed in the test results of a photocatalytic material serves as a direct measure of its photocatalytic efficiency. A greater photocurrent response indicates a more efficient process of generating, separating, and transporting electron-hole pairs that are created by light [66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance for the ciprofloxacin (CPF) and paracetamol (PCT)
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 4 displays the temporal efficiency of the comparative catalysts GQDs, ZnO/GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs/CdSe in the degrading process of CPF and PCT. Fig. 4(a) displays the results of photodegradation utilizing GQDs, ZnO/GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs/CdSe under visible light. The concentration (C/C0) of CPF after degradation is 0.79, 0.17, and 0.02, respectively. The corresponding degradation percentages are 21.20 %, 83.36 %, and 98.49 % respectively (Fig. S3(a)) with the starting concentration of CPF was 10 ppm. The UV-driven photodegradation rate constants (k) of GQDs, ZnO/GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs/CdSe photocatalysts in Fig. 4(b) were determined to be 6.02 E−4, 0.0072, and 0.0083, respectively. The decline in CPF removal efficiency was conducted every 240 min. Fig. 4(c) displays the results of the photodegradation of PCT using GQDs, ZnO/GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs/CdSe under visible light. The concentration (C/C0) of PCT after photodegradation is 0.45, 0.003, and 0.001, respectively. The corresponding degradation percentages are 55.1, 99.85, and 99.70 %, respectively (Fig. S3(b)). Once the visible light is activated, the breakdown of different pollutants becomes markedly more pronounced. The photoexcitation reaction operates by harnessing absorbed photons to create electron-hole pairs, effectively separating them via the Z-scheme mechanism, which leads to the production of reactive oxygen species that contribute to the degradation of pollutants. The initial concentration of PCT was 10 ppm. The UV-driven photodegradation rate constants (k) of GQDs, ZnO/GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs/CdSe photocatalysts were determined to be 0.002, 0.014, and 0.031, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4(d). The decline in CPF removal efficiency was conducted at intervals of 100 min. Various condition of visible light watt also has been study in this research, 15, 50 and 75 Watt, the result show in Fig. 5 , the wattage of the light source plays a crucial role in photodegradation processes by influencing factors such as light intensity, penetration depth, temperature, and energy efficiency. Fig. 5(a) The concentration (C/C0) of CPF after degradation under various watt of visible light and the result show is 0.015, 0.13, and 0.07, respectively. The corresponding degradation percentages are 98.52, 98.54, and 99.24 % (15, 50 and 75 W), respectively (Fig. S3(c)). The UV-driven photodegradation rate constants (k) of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe photocatalysts in various watt condition 15, 50, 75 Watt shown in Fig. S3(d) were determined to be 0008, 0.019, and 0.019, respectively. The farther you are from the light source, the less intense the light is. Greater penetration depth of higher wattage light sources allows for more consistent exposure and reaches more target molecules for destruction [47]. The concentration (C/C0) of PCT after degradation in various watt also has been study and its show in Fig. 5(b), the final concentration of PCT is 0.001, 0.0008, and 0. 0.0004, respectively. The corresponding degradation percentages are 99.2, 99.4, and 99.9 % (15, 50 and 75 W), respectively (Fig. S3(e)). The UV-driven photodegradation rate constants (k) of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe photocatalysts in various watt condition 15, 50 and 75 W shown in Fig. S3(f) were determined to be 0.03, 0.03, and 0.05, respectively. The effect of various pH also has been study in this research. This study demonstrates that ZnO/GQDs/CdSe exhibits significant results in comparison to the previous study (Table 4). The influence of pH on the photocatalytic degradation of the dye was examined within the pH range of 3–10. The outcomes are displayed in Fig. 5(c) for the CPF effect and Fig. 5(d) for the PCT effect. The significant increase in degradation efficiency seen at a pH range of 5 is attributed to the heightened adsorption of anionic pollutants onto the positively charged surface of the ZnO catalyst. Nevertheless, when exposed to a pH 3 that is highly acidic, ZnO has the potential to dissolve [70–74]. As a consequence, there is a significant decline in photoactivity when compared to pH 5. Under neutral to alkaline conditions (pH 8–10), the circumstances vary. The surface of ZnO possesses a negative charge, which can be determined, and this charge acts to resist the pollutant. This could lead to a reduction in the adsorption of the negatively charged pollutant on the catalyst's surface, thereby resulting in a drop in photoactivity [75–78]. The further investigation into the photodegradation of the dye will be conducted while maintaining the initial solution pH of the natural dye solution. Fig. 5(e) and (f) The impact of catalyst loading, ranging from 25 to 75 mg, on the photodegradation of Ciprofloxacin and Paracetamol was also examined, as depicted in Fig. 5(e) and (f). The inclusion of catalyst content led to an enhancement in the efficiency of photodegradation. This was primarily due to an increase in the number of pollutant molecules adsorbed on the catalyst surface and an augmentation in the density of catalyst particles in the region of photo irradiation. The degradation efficiency was significantly enhanced by increasing the catalyst content from 25 to 75 mg. This is caused by the escalation of solution turbidity following the introduction of catalyst content. A decrease in the transmission of visible light due to an increase in light scattering would occur. Hence, the amount of catalyst used was set at 50 mg for subsequent investigation. The catalyst features highly reactive photogenerated holes (h⁺) that can readily react with water or hydroxide ions (OH⁺) on its surface, resulting in the formation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH). These reactive oxygen species (ROS) subsequently target the pollutant molecules, causing their degradation into smaller, less detrimental bits. The process of photocatalytic destruction of the harmful pollutant may be described as a follow (Fig. 6 , and Fig. S4).
Image 1

In this study, the degradation mechanism of PCT and CPF was investigated using several scavengers, namely Potassium iodide (KI), Isopropyl (IPA), and benzoquinone (BQ), which function as holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and superoxide radicals (•O2–) respectively. Under the influence of KI (from 99.2 to 91.3 %), IPA (99.2–11.2 %), and BQ (99.2–22.8 %), the photocatalytic degradation of PCT was reduced upon exposure to light irradiation, as depicted in Figure S6 (a). This finding illustrates that both superoxide (•O2–) and hydroxyl (OH•) radicals are of primary importance in the breakdown of PCT, but holes (h+) do not make a substantial contribution to the photocatalytic process. The generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) by the ZnO/GQDs/CdSe photocatalyst under light irradiation is essential for the degradation of PCT. These radicals, formed by the interaction of photo-generated holes with water or hydroxide ions, exhibit great reactivity and efficiently oxidize the paracetamol molecules into smaller, less toxic chemicals. This process is enhanced by the composite photocatalyst through the improvement of light absorption, reduction of electron-hole recombination, and extension of photocatalytic activity into the visible light spectrum [80,81]. Fig. S6(b) illustrates the impact of several scavengers on the photodegradation of CPF using ZnO/GQDs/CdSe. The results indicate that KI (from 99.9 % to 83.4 %), IPA (from 99.9 % to 77.8 %), and BQ (from 99.9 % to 31.4 %) have a significant influence on the formation of superoxide radicals (•O2–) in the degradation of CPF. The ZnO/GQDs/CdSe photocatalyst utilizes superoxide radicals (•O2–) to interact with and oxidize ciprofloxacin, hence facilitating its photodegradation. The generation of these radicals occurs when photo-excited electrons from the catalyst undergo reduction of oxygen molecules, therefore producing reactive oxygen species that facilitate the breakdown of ciprofloxacin into more basic units. Fig. 7 (a) demonstrates the photocatalytic durability of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe compounds. Following each photocatalytic degradation of the PCT and CPF solution, the powder was subjected to 24 h drying process in an oven to remove all residual liquid. Subsequently, the collected catalyst powder was thoroughly rinsed multiple times with deionized water to eliminate any remaining traces of CPF and PCT from the surface of the compound. The gathered substances were subsequently reintroduced into a new PCT and CPF solution to begin a fresh experiment. In the loop experiment, all experimental settings and processes remain consistent with those of the first experiment. The stability and reusability of compounds during the degradation of CPF and PCT were investigated using three consecutive cycle tests. According to Fig. 7(a), the degradation efficiency remained consistent and did not decline considerably after 5 cycles. Additionally, the elimination rate for PCT and CPF remained above 99 %. Fig. 7(b) shows evidence of the structural stability of the synthesized catalyst, revealing the results of data on FTIR obtained before and after the photodegradation investigation showing similar results. The results of the Photocurrent Test shown in Fig. 7(c) show that the ZnO/GQDs/CdSe samples before and after have a current density that shows their stability.However, the findings are limited by the relatively short experimental duration and controlled laboratory conditions, which may not fully represent long-term real-world applications. Future studies should explore extended exposure times and environmental variables to validate these results in practical settings. Despite these limitations, the results provide valuable insight into the potential of the composite for pollutant degradation.

This study focuses on the creation and analysis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), zinc oxide/graphene quantum dots (ZnO/GQDs), and zinc oxide/graphene quantum dots/cadmium selenide (ZnO/GQDs/CdSe) composites. These composites are used as photocatalysts to break down Ciprofloxacin and Paracetamol through a process called photodegradation. The existence of ZnO/GQDs/CdSe was confirmed using a range of characterization methods, such as TEM, SEM, EDX, XRD, XPS, and DRS-UV–vis spectroscopy. The electrochemical performance was assessed using various techniques including Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV), Tafel plots, Chronoamperometry, Cyclic Voltammetry, and Photocurrent Response. The photocatalytic degradation of PCT and CPF showed an implantation rate of 99.2 % and 99.9 % respectively. The study investigated the influence of experimental factors, including catalyst dosage recycling, pH levels, and various wattages of light sources, on the photocatalytic activity.

David Nugroho: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Latiful Kabir: Data curation. Young Jun Joo: Formal analysis. Kwang Youn Cho: Data curation. Suwat Nanan: Conceptualization. Saksit Chanthai: Formal analysis. Rachadaporn Benchawattananon: Conceptualization. Won-Chun Oh: Writing – review & editing.
